critic_raw:
This design compares a bidirectional LSTM against logistic regression for predictive maintenance (equipment failure prediction) using 36 months of SCADA sensor data.

The RNN uses a 168-hour (7-day) lookback window, and the positive label is defined as sensor sequences occurring within 168 hours before a logged failure. This means the lookback window and the label definition window are identical in length. Any sequence in the positive class will, by construction, contain readings from the run-to-failure period — the sensor values during active degradation. The model is therefore partially learning to distinguish degradation sensor signatures from normal operation, which may be achievable simply from statistical properties of the final hours before failure rather than long-range sequential patterns. The LSTM's claimed advantage is temporal sequential modeling, but if the predictive signal is concentrated in the last few hours of the 168-hour window, a simpler model operating on recent sensor statistics would perform equally well. No experiment is described to test whether temporal depth beyond a short window contributes.

The logistic regression baseline receives the same 9 engineered features 'averaged or summarized over the 168-hour window to produce a fixed-length feature vector (9 features).' Averaging over 168 hours for the baseline while providing the full 168-step sequence to the LSTM is not equivalent feature access — it is a deliberate information degradation. Rolling statistics and rate-of-change features already capture temporal information at the feature level; a logistic regression on the full set of per-timestep features (or at least on features computed at multiple time scales) would be a more informative baseline.

F1 score is used as the tuning metric on the validation set. F1 depends on the decision threshold, and the threshold that maximizes F1 on validation may not be appropriate for the test set if the positive class prevalence differs. F1 optimization implicitly assumes the prevalence on the validation set is representative of the deployment prevalence. In equipment failure prediction, failure rates vary by machine type, age, and usage intensity; if the validation period happens to have different failure rates than the test period, the threshold is miscalibrated. The design does not report whether failure rates are consistent across all three time windows.
all_issues_raised: The 168-hour lookback window is identical to the 168-hour pre-failure label window, meaning positive sequences contain active degradation readings throughout; the model may achieve high performance from recent-hours signal alone, not from long-range sequential patterns.

The logistic regression baseline receives a 9-feature summary averaged over the 168-hour window while the LSTM receives the full 168-timestep sequence — this information asymmetry prevents isolating the architectural contribution of sequential modeling.

F1 is optimized on the validation set to select the decision threshold; if failure rate differs between validation and test periods, the threshold is miscalibrated for test evaluation, and the design does not verify failure rate consistency across time windows.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 168-hour lookback window is identical to the 168-hour pre-failure label window, meaning positive sequences contain active degradation readings throughout; the model may achieve high performance from recent-hours signal alone, not from long-range sequential patterns.

The logistic regression baseline receives a 9-feature summary averaged over the 168-hour window while the LSTM receives the full 168-timestep sequence — this information asymmetry prevents isolating the architectural contribution of sequential modeling.

F1 is optimized on the validation set to select the decision threshold; if failure rate differs between validation and test periods, the threshold is miscalibrated for test evaluation, and the design does not verify failure rate consistency across time windows.
